seg = current_frame.segmentation
nodes = seg['nodes']

# Find all colored diamonds

        print(f"id={n['id']}, color={n['color']}, pixels={n['pixels']}, boundary={n['boundary']}")

# Check the diamond area

print("\n=== Diamond area (rows 50-62, cols 24-34) ===")
for r in range(50, 62):

# Also check ring area

for r in range(17, 25):
    print(f"Row {r}: {ascii_lines[r][35:43]}")
</parameter>

sections.label: THINKING
sections.content:
Interesting! After clicking the gray diamond:
1. The gray diamond at the bottom (rows 59-61) turned into a WHITE diamond! It now has color W with 8 pixels.
2. The white diamond that was at (52-54, 27-30) turned into a GRAY diamond (color G, 6 pixels)!
3. The purple diamond stayed the same.
So clicking the gray diamond caused a color swap: the gray diamond became white, and the white diamond became gray. This is a color transfer!
Looking at the pattern:
- The purple diamond moved toward the gray diamond (absorbing it somehow)
- The white diamond that was trailing behind got the gray color
- The gray diamond at the end got the white color
So the colors are being transferred along the chain. The purple diamond is the "carrier" that moves along the path, and when it reaches the end, it triggers a color swap between the start and end of the chain.
Now I have:
- Ring (empty, purple)
- Gray diamond at (52-54, 27-30) - was white
- Purple diamond at (54-58, 25-29) - unchanged
- White diamond at (59-61, 25-29) - was gray
Maybe I need to now move the gray diamond (which was white) back toward the ring? Let me try clicking on the gray diamond at its new position.

Plan: Try clicking the gray diamond to move it back toward the ring.
